questions.question: Explain the concept of unfolding an RNN and why it is necessary for backpropagation.
keyPoints: RNN has cyclic connections that prevent direct gradient computation
Unfolding creates a directed acyclic graph with copies of network for each time step
All time steps share the same weight parameters
This converts recurrent computation into equivalent feedforward computation

questions.question: What is the vanishing gradient problem in BPTT? Explain why it occurs.
keyPoints: Gradients become exponentially small as they propagate backward through time
Caused by repeated multiplication of small derivatives (tanh/sigmoid) and weight matrices
Makes it difficult for RNN to learn long-term dependencies
Gradient contributions from distant time steps become negligible

questions.question: Differentiate between BPTT and Truncated BPTT. When would you prefer each?
keyPoints: BPTT computes gradients through all time steps in the sequence
Truncated BPTT limits backpropagation to k recent time steps
Truncated BPTT is computationally cheaper and mitigates vanishing gradients
Use full BPTT for short sequences, Truncated for very long sequences

questions.question: List and explain the learnable parameters in a simple Elman RNN.
keyPoints: W_xh: Input to hidden weights
W_hh: Hidden to hidden (recurrent) weights
W_hy: Hidden to output weights
b_h: Hidden layer bias
b_y: Output layer bias
All weights are shared across time steps

questions.question: How does gradient clipping help in training RNNs? Explain with the method.
keyPoints: Solves exploding gradient problem in BPTT
Compute gradient norm ||g||
If ||g|| > threshold, scale gradient: g = (threshold/||g||) * g
Preserves gradient direction while limiting magnitude

questions.question: Write the equations for forward pass in a simple RNN and explain each component.
keyPoints: h_t = tanh(W_xh * x_t + W_hh * h_{t-1} + b_h)
y_t = W_hy * h_t + b_y
h_t: current hidden state, x_t: current input
h_{t-1}: previous hidden state (memory)
tanh squashes values to [-1,1]

questions.question: Explain the computational complexity of BPTT and why it can be expensive.
keyPoints: Time complexity O(τ × n) where τ is sequence length, n is parameters
Forward pass: O(τ) to compute all hidden states
Backward pass: O(τ) to propagate gradients
Memory: O(τ) to store all intermediate activations

questions.question: Derive the gradient equations for the recurrent weight matrix W_hh in BPTT for a simple RNN. Show how the gradient is computed across multiple time steps.
keyPoints: Start with loss L = Σ_t l_t
Use chain rule: ∂L/∂W_hh = Σ_t ∂L/∂h_t × ∂h_t/∂W_hh
∂h_t/∂W_hh includes contributions from all future time steps
Need to sum gradients from each time step where W_hh appears in unfolded network
Show recursive gradient computation: ∂L/∂h_t = (∂L/∂h_{t+1} × W_hh) × f'(net_t)
Discuss accumulation of gradients across time

questions.question: Compare BPTT with Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL). Discuss their advantages and disadvantages in terms of computational complexity, memory requirements, and suitability for different sequence lengths.
keyPoints: BPTT: O(τn²) complexity, high memory (store all activations)
RTRL: O(n³) complexity per update, lower memory
BPTT is offline (requires full sequence), RTRL is online
For long sequences, BPTT truncated or RTRL preferred
BPTT better for short sequences with known length
Trade-off between memory and computation

questions.question: Explain the exploding gradient problem in BPTT. How does it manifest during training? Discuss gradient clipping as a solution and provide the algorithm.
keyPoints: Gradients grow exponentially when propagating backward through time
Occurs when weight matrices have eigenvalues > 1
Manifests as NaN values or very large weight updates
Gradient clipping algorithm: compute gradient g, if ||g|| > δ, scale to δ
Preserves direction, limits magnitude
Alternative solutions: LSTM/GRU architectures, proper initialization

questions.question: How do LSTM networks address the limitations of BPTT for learning long-term dependencies? Explain the gating mechanism and how it helps in gradient flow.
keyPoints: LSTM uses forget, input, and output gates
Gates control information flow through time steps
Forget gate: decides what to discard from memory
Input gate: decides what new information to store
Cell state: linear recurrence (addition) allows gradient to flow unchanged
Sigmoid outputs between 0-1, multiplied with tanh outputs
This gating prevents gradient vanishing through additive updates
Enables learning of dependencies across thousands of time steps
